aliases.name: 八岐大蛇
aliases.meaning: Eight-branching great serpent; the kanji rendering combining eight (八), branch/fork (岐), great (大), serpent (蛇)

aliases.name: 八俣遠呂知
aliases.meaning: Alternative archaic kanji rendering found in the Kojiki

aliases.name: Orochi
aliases.meaning: Great serpent; the short form used independently in later tradition

description.summary: An eight-headed, eight-tailed giant serpent of Japanese mythology, slain by the storm god Susanoo no Mikoto in the Hi River valley of Izumo Province. The creature's defeat yielded the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi sword, one of Japan's three Imperial Regalia, making the Orochi myth one of the most consequential narratives in Japanese culture.
description.full: Yamata no Orochi (八岐大蛇, 'eight-branching great serpent') is the most famous monster of Japanese mythology, recorded in Japan's two oldest written texts: the Kojiki (712 CE) and the Nihon Shoki (720 CE). According to the Kojiki, the serpent had eight heads and eight tails, eyes like red cherries, a body covered in moss, cypress, and cedar, spanning eight valleys and eight peaks, with a belly perpetually oozing blood. The storm deity Susanoo no Mikoto encountered the grieving couple Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi, who had lost seven daughters to the serpent's annual appetite; only Kushinada-hime remained. Susanoo defeated the creature through stratagem: he transformed Kushinada-hime into a comb hidden in his hair, instructed the parents to brew eightfold-refined sake in eight vessels, and hid these behind a fence. The Orochi, discovering the sake, drank from each vessel with one of its heads and fell into a drunken stupor. Susanoo then cut the creature apart with his Totsuka-no-Tsurugi sword. When he severed the middle tail, his blade met resistance and broke — revealing the legendary Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi ('Grass-Cutting Sword') hidden within the serpent's flesh. This sword was subsequently presented to Amaterasu, the sun goddess, and became one of Japan's three Imperial Regalia. Scholars have proposed that the Orochi myth encodes historical realities: the serpent may represent the Hi River's flooding, the narrative may reflect ancient conflict between Izumo Province culture and the encroaching Yamato state, and the metallurgical theme (the sword emerging from the serpent) may reflect the spread of iron-smelting technology from the Korean peninsula.

etymology.origin: Old Japanese; documented in the Kojiki (712 CE)
etymology.meaning: Yamata (八岐/八俣): eight-forked or eight-branched. No: possessive or descriptive particle. Orochi: great serpent, from Old Japanese woröti; possibly combining ō (great) with chi (god/spirit) or dialectal oro (peak/tail).
etymology.first_use: 712 CE (Kojiki); the myth predates the written record in oral tradition

related_phenomena.name: Lernaean Hydra
related_phenomena.description: Multi-headed serpent slain by a hero in Greek mythology; scholars note cross-cultural parallels with the Orochi myth

related_phenomena.name: Nāga
related_phenomena.description: Buddhist serpent deity; medieval Buddhist scholars identified Yamata no Orochi with the Nāga Maiden of the Lotus Sutra

related_phenomena.name: Ibuki Daimyōjin
related_phenomena.description: Mountain deity of Mount Ibuki; Shinto tradition holds that the surviving Orochi spirit fled to Mount Ibuki and was enshrined there

related_phenomena.name: Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi
related_phenomena.description: The legendary sword revealed within the Orochi's middle tail; one of Japan's three Imperial Regalia

features.description: The serpent's defining feature: eight separate heads, each capable of drinking independently from a separate sake vessel
Eight tails; Susanoo's blade broke on the middle tail, revealing the hidden sword
Described in the Kojiki as having eyes 'like red cherries' (red, glossy, intensely colored)

features.feature: Body covered in moss, cypress, and cedar
features.description: The Kojiki describes the creature's back and flanks as overgrown with vegetation, suggesting immense age and size
The creature's belly reportedly oozed blood continuously according to the Kojiki account

features.feature: Spans eight valleys and eight peaks
features.description: The creature's body is described as so vast it spans eight valleys and eight peaks simultaneously, making it a landscape-scale being

physical.morphology: A colossal eight-headed, eight-tailed serpentine creature of landscape scale, its body draped in forest vegetation suggesting geological age. The eight-fold structure (eight heads, eight tails, eight valleys, eight peaks) reflects ancient Japanese cosmological numerology representing overwhelming, multiplied power.

abilities.description: The Orochi consumed one daughter of the Ashinazuchi-Tenazuchi family each year for seven successive years before being slain
The creature's sheer scale — spanning eight valleys and eight peaks — implies control over or embodiment of a large geographical area
Within the Orochi's middle tail, the legendary sword Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi was hidden — suggesting the creature was itself a vessel or guardian of divine power

habitat_preference: Izumo Province (Hi River valley, Shimane Prefecture)
behavior.diet: Human victims; the myth describes annual consumption of young women
patterns: Annual arrival at the Hi River valley to consume one daughter
Susceptible to sake; the entire plot resolution depends on its inability to resist the eightfold-refined sake
Associated with the Hi River, which may represent the flooding river that the myth encodes

range.description: The myth is geographically anchored to the Hi River (now the Hii River) in Izumo Province (modern Shimane Prefecture), western Honshu, Japan. The narrative is national in significance but locally specific in setting.

hotspots.name: Hii River (Hi River / 簸川), Shimane Prefecture

location.description: The Hi River (now Hii River) in Izumo Province, Shimane Prefecture, western Honshu, Japan; the mythological setting of Susanoo's encounter with and slaying of the Orochi

hotspots.notes: The Izumo no Kuni Fudoki identifies the Nita District nearby as an ancient iron-production center, supporting metallurgical interpretations of the myth

hotspots.name: Mount Ibuki (Ibukiyama), Shiga/Gifu Prefectures

location.description: Mountain on the Shiga-Gifu border, Japan; where the surviving Orochi spirit is said to have fled and been enshrined as Ibuki Daimyōjin

hotspots.notes: Ibuki Daimyōjin is the deified form of the surviving Orochi spirit; the same deity is credited in the Kojiki with killing the hero Yamato Takeru through illness

first_recorded.source: Kojiki (712 CE), compiled by Ō no Yasumaro under imperial order

origins.cultural_roots: The Yamata no Orochi myth belongs to the Izumo tradition, one of the two major regional mythological complexes of ancient Japan (the other being the Yamato tradition). Izumo Province (modern Shimane Prefecture) had a distinct cultural identity, and the Orochi narrative may encode historical tension between Izumo and the expanding Yamato state, represented by Susanoo — a deity who was expelled from Takamagahara (the High Plain of Heaven) before arriving in Izumo.
origins.earliest_accounts: Kojiki (712 CE), the oldest extant chronicle of Japan, compiled under Emperor Genmei. The Nihon Shoki (720 CE) provides six variant retellings of the same core narrative.
origins.folklore_connections: Scholar Ōbayashi Taryō has linked the dragon-slayer motif to cross-cultural parallels including Perseus and Cetus, suggesting possible transmission via southern Korea or the Yangzi River region. Medieval Buddhist scholarship identified the Orochi with the Nāga Maiden of the Lotus Sutra.

timeline.event: Kojiki compiled; contains the foundational account of Susanoo's slaying of Yamata no Orochi and discovery of the Kusanagi sword
timeline.significance: Oldest written record of the myth; canonical text of Japanese mythology

timeline.event: Nihon Shoki compiled; contains six variant retellings of the Orochi myth with additional detail and alternate names for the sword (Ama-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi)
timeline.significance: Second canonical source; provides alternate names and interpretive variants

timeline.event: Battle of Dan-no-Ura; the Kusanagi sword, one of the Imperial Regalia recovered from the Orochi, sinks into the sea when Emperor Antoku drowns; the Heike Monogatari later proposes this was Orochi's reincarnation reclaiming the sword
timeline.significance: Historical event that prompted mythological interpretation connecting the Orochi myth to the Genpei War
timeline.source: Heike Monogatari (compiled 1371 CE by Akashi Kakuichi)

timeline.event: Akashi Kakuichi compiles the Heike Monogatari in its canonical form, including the account proposing Yamata no Orochi reincarnated as Emperor Antoku
timeline.significance: Demonstrates the myth's continued interpretive power in medieval Japan

timeline.event: Film The Little Prince and the Eight-Headed Dragon released; early major modern cinematic treatment of the Orochi myth
timeline.source: The Little Prince and the Eight-Headed Dragon (1963, Toei Animation)

history.modern_era: Yamata no Orochi remains one of the most culturally active mythological figures in Japan. Shimane Prefecture uses the Orochi name for local landmarks and infrastructure (Okuizumo Orochi Loop highway, Orochi Street in Izumo City). The Shimane Susanoo Magic basketball team and Iwami Kagura folk theater performances keep the myth in active cultural life. The Orochi has been adapted in countless modern media: manga, anime, video games (Okami, King of Fighters), and literature.

documentary.name: Kojiki (Record of Ancient Matters)
documentary.description: Japan's oldest chronicle, compiled in 712 CE under imperial order. Contains the foundational narrative of Susanoo's encounter with and slaying of Yamata no Orochi, and the discovery of the Kusanagi sword.
documentary.analysis: The Kojiki is the primary written source for the Orochi myth. As a court-compiled document, it reflects the official mythological and political narrative of the Yamato court.

documentary.name: Nihon Shoki (Chronicles of Japan)
documentary.description: Japan's second oldest chronicle (720 CE), providing six variant retellings of the Orochi myth and naming the sword Ama-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi.
documentary.analysis: The multiple variant retellings in the Nihon Shoki indicate the myth was already diversified in oral tradition before codification.

scientific.name: Hydrological / river-flood encoding
scientific.description: Scholars including Miura Sukeyuki propose that Yamata no Orochi personifies the Hi River in flood, which annually devastated the valley. The eight heads represent the river's tributaries or multiple flood channels; Susanoo's defeat of the serpent represents human mastery of water management and flood control.
evidence_cited: The myth is set on the Hi River (now Hii River), known for flooding
Annual predation parallels annual flood damage
The Hi River's tributaries form an eight-branched delta

scientific.name: Metallurgical / iron-technology encoding
scientific.description: Alternative analysis proposes the narrative reflects the introduction of iron-smelting from the Korean peninsula. The serpent represents a mountain spirit controlling iron-sand-bearing flood deposits; Susanoo (associated with the iron-producing area of Susa in Iishi District) defeats it, and the sword emerging from the creature symbolizes the mastery of iron technology. The Nita District nearby is documented as an ancient iron-production center.
evidence_cited: Izumo no Kuni Fudoki identifies the Nita District as an ancient iron-production center
Susa (Susanoo's associated place) was an iron-producing area in Iishi District
The sword emerging from the serpent symbolizes technological mastery over nature

scientific.name: Political conflict encoding (Izumo vs. Yamato)
scientific.description: The myth may encode historical conflict between the politically distinct Izumo culture and the encroaching Yamato imperial state. Susanoo, exiled from the heavenly realm, arrives in Izumo and kills its most powerful creature — a possible mythological representation of Yamato political and military expansion over the Izumo region.
proponents: Historians of ancient Japanese political consolidation
evidence_cited: Susanoo is a figure expelled from the Yamato-aligned heaven (Takamagahara) before his Izumo adventure
The Kusanagi sword — the primary outcome — is immediately gifted to the Yamato sun goddess Amaterasu
Izumo Province maintained a distinct political identity prior to Yamato consolidation

scientific.name: Comparative dragon-slayer mythology
scientific.description: Scholar Ōbayashi Taryō links the Orochi myth to a widespread cross-cultural dragon-slayer archetype, including Perseus and Cetus in Greek mythology, suggesting possible transmission through southern Korea or the Yangzi River cultural corridor.
evidence_cited: Structural parallels with Greek Perseus/Cetus and other dragon-slayer myths
Cultural contact routes via the Korean peninsula
Eight-headed serpent motifs in Korean and Chinese mythology

traditions: Iwami Kagura folk theater tradition (Orochi slaying as most popular performance)
stories: Kojiki narrative (712 CE): Susanoo encounters grieving family, transforms Kushinada-hime into a comb, defeats Orochi with eightfold sake, discovers Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi in the creature's middle tail
Ibuki Daimyōjin tradition: the surviving Orochi spirit fled to Mount Ibuki and was enshrined as a protective mountain deity; later caused the death of the hero Yamato Takeru through illness
Heike Monogatari interpretation (1371 CE): Yamata no Orochi reincarnated as Emperor Antoku to reclaim the Kusanagi sword, which sank with Antoku at Dan-no-Ura (1185)
folklore.beliefs: The Orochi represents the archetypal force of chaos and destruction overcome by divine heroism. The eight-fold structure (heads, tails, valleys, peaks) encodes the ancient Japanese cosmological significance of eight as a number of multiplied power. The discovery of the Kusanagi sword within the creature's body implies that order and civilization — represented by the sword — were always latent within the chaos the Orochi embodied.

literature: Heike Monogatari (compiled 1371 CE) — interprets the Battle of Dan-no-Ura through the Orochi myth
film_tv: The Little Prince and the Eight-Headed Dragon (1963, Toei Animation)
games: Okami (Capcom, 2006) — Orochi as the central villain
The King of Fighters series (SNK) — Orochi as major antagonist
Numerous additional video games featuring the Orochi or Yamata no Orochi as bosses or lore elements
other: Okuizumo Orochi Loop — scenic mountain highway in Shimane Prefecture named for the creature
Orochi Street in Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture
Shimane Susanoo Magic — professional basketball team named for the deity who slew the Orochi
Iwami Kagura folk theater — the Orochi-slaying is the most popular performance in this regional tradition

researchers.name: Miura Sukeyuki
researchers.contribution: Hydrological interpretation of the Orochi myth as encoding Hi River flood dynamics

researchers.name: Ōbayashi Taryō
researchers.contribution: Linked the Orochi dragon-slayer myth to cross-cultural parallels including Greek mythology; proposed transmission via southern Korea or the Yangzi River region

research.ongoing_studies: The Orochi myth continues to be studied in Japanese mythology, comparative mythology, and the archaeology of ancient Izumo Province. Ongoing work explores the relationship between the myth and ancient iron production in Shimane Prefecture.
